summary: The transformation rule appears to be that for any given cell in the input grid, if its value is not zero, it gets replaced by the minimum value of its neighboring cells (horizontally, vertically, but not diagonally) that are not zero. If a cell is zero and has no non-zero neighbors, it remains zero. If a cell is zero but has non-zero neighbors, it does not change. For cells that are on the border and have no non-zero neighbors, they remain the same. For non-zero cells surrounded by zeros, they change to the value of their neighbor if there's only one type of neighbor value.
Rule: Replace non-zero cells with the minimum non-zero value of their horizontal and vertical neighbors.

summary: The transformation rule appears to be that for any given cell in the input grid, if the cell's value is not zero and it is surrounded by cells of the same value (horizontally, vertically, or both), then all cells of the same value that are connected to it (forming a contiguous region) are set to the same value in the output grid. Otherwise, if the cell is not surrounded by the same value or is on the edge, its value does not change unless it becomes part of a new contiguous region of the same value. Essentially, it seems that regions of the same non-zero value that are not isolated become filled with that value if they are not already.
Rule: Contiguous region fill

summary: The transformation rule appears to be that for any input grid cell that is not 0, if all its neighboring cells (horizontally, vertically, and diagonally) have the same value as the cell or are 0, then the output grid cell retains its value. Otherwise, it takes the value of the smallest non-zero neighboring cell if such neighbors exist, or remains unchanged if only zero neighbors exist. However, a simpler and more consistent rule derived from the examples given is that any input cell value that is surrounded (horizontally and vertically, not diagonally) by cells of the same value or zero, will propagate its value to the output cell if it is not isolated. Isolated cells or cells on the border with mixed neighbors tend to keep their original value unless a smaller value is nearby.
Rule: Cells surrounded by same or zero values propagate, otherwise take smallest nearby value.
